1: "LORD, who may dwell on your holy mountain?" — the entrance liturgy's question, which Christ alone answers from his own nature and which his people answer through union with him. Hebrews 12:22-24: "You have come to Mount Zion, to the city of the living God, the heavenly Jerusalem... to Jesus the mediator of a new covenant." Access to the holy mountain is through Christ the mediator. He is simultaneously the answer to the question (he alone qualifies perfectly) and the one through whom others are qualified.
2: "The one whose walk is blameless, who does what is righteous, who speaks the truth from their heart" — the three qualifications Christ alone perfectly met. "Blameless (tāmîm)" — Christ was without sin (Hebrews 4:15; 2 Corinthians 5:21). "Does what is righteous" — "the Righteous One" is a Messianic title (Acts 3:14; Psalms 7:52; Psalms 22:14). "Speaks the truth from their heart" — Christ is "the truth" in his very person (John 14:6), the one in whom word and heart are perfectly aligned. These qualities describe the covenant-keeper par excellence.
3: "...whose tongue utters no slander, who does no evil to a neighbor, and casts no slur on others" — Christ perfectly fulfilled each of these. He spoke no slander. He did no evil to any neighbor — even his trial and crucifixion were initiated and carried out by others. 1 Peter 2:22-23: "He committed no sin, and no deceit was found in his mouth. When they hurled their insults at him, he did not retaliate; when he suffered, he made no threats." The standard of Psalms 15:3 is met only in the one who bore insult without returning it.
4: "...who despises a vile person but honors those who fear the LORD; who keeps an oath even when it hurts, and does not change their mind" — Christ kept his covenant oath at infinite personal cost. "Having loved his own who were in the world, he loved them to the end" (John 13:1). The garden prayer "if it is possible, may this cup be taken from me" was the voice of the cost; "yet not as I will, but as you will" (Matthew 26:39) was the oath kept regardless. He "endured the cross, scorning its shame" (Hebrews 12:2) — the ultimate oath-keeping at infinite cost.
5: "...who lends money to the poor without interest; who does not accept a bribe against the innocent. Whoever does these things will never be shaken" — Christ, who could not be bribed (the thirty pieces of silver were paid to Judas to betray him, not to him to betray others), who charged no one for healing, who gave freely to all who came — embodies the economic justice described here. "Never be shaken (lōʾ yimmôṭ)" — the promise of unshakeable security for those who dwell on the holy mountain through Christ (Hebrews 12:27-28: we are "receiving a kingdom that cannot be shaken").
